subUnits.title: 9.1 Common Indicators and Their pH Ranges
notes: Acid-Base Indicators:
• Substances that change color depending on the pH of the solution
• Used to determine the endpoint in titrations and estimate pH
• Most indicators are weak organic acids or bases
Common Indicators and Their Color Changes:
1. Litmus:
• Red in acid (pH < 4.5), Blue in base (pH > 8.3)
• Purple at neutral (pH ≈ 7)
• Natural indicator from lichens
2. Methyl Orange:
• Red in acid (pH < 3.1), Yellow in base (pH > 4.4)
• Orange at transition (pH 3.1–4.4)
• Used for strong acid-weak base titrations
3. Phenolphthalein:
• Colorless in acid (pH < 8.2), Pink in base (pH > 10.0)
• Used for strong base-weak acid titrations
4. Bromothymol Blue:
• Yellow in acid (pH < 6.0), Blue in base (pH > 7.6)
• Green at neutral (pH ≈ 7)
• Useful for detecting neutralization
5. Universal Indicator:
• Mixture of several indicators
• Shows a spectrum of colors across pH 1–14
• Red (pH 1) → Orange → Yellow → Green (pH 7) → Blue → Purple (pH 14)
pH Scale:
• pH = -log₁₀[H⁺], where [H⁺] is hydrogen ion concentration in mol/dm³
• pH < 7: Acidic, pH = 7: Neutral, pH > 7: Basic/Alkaline
• Each pH unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]
Choosing the Right Indicator:
• Strong acid + Strong base: Any indicator (pH change is sharp, 3–11)
• Strong acid + Weak base: Methyl orange (endpoint pH ≈ 4–6)
• Weak acid + Strong base: Phenolphthalein (endpoint pH ≈ 8–10)
• Weak acid + Weak base: No sharp endpoint; use pH meter instead

quiz.q: An acid-base indicator changes color because:
opts: It is a weak acid/base whose conjugate forms have different colors
quiz.explanation: Indicators are weak acids/bases; the acid and conjugate base forms have different colors, and the ratio shifts with pH.

quiz.q: Phenolphthalein is colorless in:
quiz.explanation: Phenolphthalein is colorless below pH 8.2 (acidic/neutral) and pink above pH 10 (basic).

quiz.q: Methyl orange is suitable for titrating:
quiz.explanation: Methyl orange changes color in the acidic range (pH 3.1–4.4), ideal for strong acid-weak base titrations.

quiz.q: The pH of a neutral solution at 25°C is:
quiz.explanation: At 25°C, pure water has [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = 1×10⁻⁷ M, so pH = -log(1×10⁻⁷) = 7.

quiz.q: A solution with pH 3 has [H⁺] that is:
quiz.explanation: pH is logarithmic: pH 3 → [H⁺] = 10⁻³ M; pH 4 → [H⁺] = 10⁻⁴ M; ratio = 10.

quiz.q: Universal indicator is:
quiz.explanation: Universal indicator is a blend of multiple indicators to show a continuous color spectrum across pH 1–14.

quiz.q: Bromothymol blue is green at approximately pH:
quiz.explanation: Bromothymol blue transitions from yellow (acid) to blue (base) with green at neutral pH ≈ 7.

quiz.q: For titrating ethanoic acid (weak) with NaOH (strong), the best indicator is:
quiz.explanation: Weak acid-strong base titrations have endpoints in the basic range (pH 8–10), where phenolphthalein changes color.

quiz.q: If [H⁺] = 1×10⁻⁵ M, the pH is:
quiz.explanation: pH = -log₁₀[H⁺] = -log(1×10⁻⁵) = 5.

quiz.q: Litmus paper turns red when dipped in:
quiz.explanation: Litmus turns red in acidic solutions (pH < 4.5); HCl is a strong acid.

subUnits.title: 9.2 Simple Acid-Base Titrations
notes: Titration: A quantitative analytical technique to determine the concentration of an unknown solution by reacting it with a standard solution of known concentration.
Apparatus for Acid-Base Titration:
1. Burette: Delivers variable volumes of titrant (standard solution) with 0.1 cm³ precision
2. Pipette: Delivers a fixed, accurate volume of analyte (unknown solution)
3. Conical flask: Holds the analyte; swirled during titration
4. White tile: Provides contrast to see color change clearly
5. Indicator: Shows endpoint by color change
6. Retort stand and clamp: Holds burette vertically
Titration Procedure (Step-by-Step):
1. Rinse burette with titrant, fill, and record initial volume
2. Pipette exact volume (e.g., 25.0 cm³) of analyte into conical flask
3. Add 2–3 drops of suitable indicator
4. Place flask on white tile under burette
5. Add titrant slowly while swirling flask
6. Near endpoint, add titrant dropwise
7. Stop when permanent color change occurs (endpoint)
8. Record final burette reading
9. Calculate titre volume = Final – Initial reading
10. Repeat until concordant titres (within 0.1 cm³) are obtained
Calculations in Titration:
General Formula: (CₐVₐ)/nₐ = (C_bV_b)/n_b
Where: C = concentration (mol/dm³), V = volume (cm³ or dm³), n = mole ratio from balanced equation
Steps:
1. Write balanced equation for the reaction
2. Determine mole ratio (nₐ:n_b)
3. Convert volumes to dm³ if needed (÷1000)
4. Substitute known values into formula
5. Solve for unknown concentration
Example Calculation:
25.0 cm³ of NaOH solution required 22.5 cm³ of 0.100 mol/dm³ HCl for neutralization.
Equation: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O (1:1 ratio)
Sources of Error & Precautions:
• Parallax error: Read burette at eye level
• Air bubbles in burette jet: Remove before starting
• Overshooting endpoint: Add titrant dropwise near endpoint
• Indicator choice: Select indicator with endpoint matching equivalence point
• Temperature: Perform at room temperature; volume changes with T
Applications:
• Determining concentration of acids/bases in industry
• Quality control in food, pharmaceutical, and water treatment
• Environmental monitoring (acid rain, water acidity)

quiz.q: In a titration, the solution of known concentration is called the:
quiz.explanation: The titrant is the solution of known concentration delivered from the burette to react with the analyte.

quiz.q: The endpoint in a titration is indicated by:
opts: A permanent color change of the indicator
quiz.explanation: The endpoint is the point at which the indicator changes color permanently, signaling completion of the reaction.

quiz.q: Concordant titres are those that agree within:
quiz.explanation: Concordant titres are replicate measurements that agree within ±0.1 cm³, ensuring precision.

quiz.q: In the formula (CₐVₐ)/nₐ = (C_bV_b)/n_b, 'n' represents:
quiz.explanation: 'n' is the stoichiometric coefficient (mole ratio) from the balanced chemical equation.

quiz.q: 25.0 cm³ of KOH required 20.0 cm³ of 0.150 mol/dm³ H₂SO₄. [KOH] is:
To avoid parallax error when reading a burette, you should:
opts: Read at eye level with the meniscus
quiz.explanation: Reading at eye level with the bottom of the meniscus prevents parallax error and ensures accuracy.

quiz.q: Phenolphthalein is used as an indicator in titrations involving:
opts: Strong acid and strong base only
quiz.explanation: Phenolphthalein changes color in the basic range (pH 8.2–10), suitable for weak acid-strong base titrations.

quiz.q: The volume delivered from a burette is calculated as:
quiz.explanation: Titre volume = Final burette reading – Initial burette reading.

quiz.q: A common source of error in titration is:
quiz.explanation: Overshooting the endpoint by adding titrant too quickly leads to inaccurate titre volumes.

quiz.q: In titration calculations, volumes in cm³ must be converted to dm³ by:
quiz.explanation: 1 dm³ = 1000 cm³, so divide cm³ by 1000 to get dm³ for use with mol/dm³ concentration units.
